---
title: "Inspecting, Selecting, and Cleaning"
description: "Understand DataFrames, choose relevant data, and handle missing values."
execute:
  echo: true
categories: [data-science]
order: 590
colab_notebook: "notebooks/lessons/data-science/inspecting-selecting-cleaning.ipynb"
---
::: {.lesson-meta}
- **Level:** Beginner to intermediate
- **Estimated time:** 35–50 minutes
- **You will learn:** Understand DataFrames, choose relevant data, and handle missing values.
- **Practice in:** Jupyter or Google Colab
:::

{{< include ../_includes/colab-link.qmd >}}

<!-- fcpython-lab: start -->
## Questions

- What problem does **Inspecting, Selecting, and Cleaning** help us solve in a small Python program?
- What should we predict before running the example?
- What value, output, or error should we inspect after changing one line?

## Objectives

- Run a complete example for **inspecting, selecting, and cleaning data** in Colab.
- Explain the example line by line using plain language.
- Change one part of the code and predict the result before running it.
- Recognize one common mistake and use the error message as evidence.

## Hands-on episode: Inspecting, Selecting, and Cleaning

Inspection reveals structure and quality. Selection focuses on relevant rows and columns. Cleaning fixes or documents issues such as missing values, inconsistent types, and impossible values.

We will learn this by running code, not by memorizing a definition first.
Open the Colab notebook from the button above, find this section, and run each
cell in order. Keep a small note beside the notebook with three columns:
**prediction**, **actual result**, and **what changed**.

### Example 1.1

Predict which rows remain after selecting scores that are not missing.

```python
import pandas as pd

data = pd.DataFrame({"name": ["Ada", "Grace", "Lin"], "score": [9, None, 7]})
clean = data.dropna(subset=["score"])
print(clean)
```

Run the cell once without editing it. If the result is different from your
prediction, leave the prediction visible and write one sentence about the
difference. That sentence is more useful than a perfect first guess.

### Explain Example 1.1

- The original data has one missing score.
- `dropna(subset=["score"])` removes rows where the score column is missing.
- The cleaned DataFrame keeps Ada and Lin but removes Grace for this specific analysis.

Now explain the example out loud or in a Markdown cell. Use short sentences:
"this line creates...", "this name stores...", "this output appears because...".
If you cannot explain a line yet, run only the lines above it and inspect the
values that exist at that moment.

### Challenge 1.1

::: {.callout-note title="Challenge"}
Instead of dropping the row, fill the missing score with a value and explain why that may or may not be honest. Cleaning choices should match the question.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show a safe way to approach the challenge</summary>

1. Copy Example 1.1 into a new Colab cell.
2. Change exactly one value, name, condition, or line.
3. Write the expected output before running the cell.
4. Run the cell and compare the actual result with your prediction.
5. If the result surprises you, undo the change and try a smaller one.

Suggested first move: Instead of dropping the row, fill the missing score with a value and explain why that may or may not be honest.

</details>

### Debugging checkpoint 1.1

::: {.callout-warning title="Debugging checkpoint"}
Do not drop missing data automatically. Ask what missing means, how many values are missing, and whether removal changes the population you are studying. Also be careful with chained indexing; use clear `.loc` selections when assigning.
:::

Do not debug by rewriting the whole example. Read the error type or surprising
output, inspect the closest value with `print(...)` or `type(...)`, then change
one thing. This is the same routine you will use in larger projects.

### Apply it

Inspect a small DataFrame with `.shape`, `.head()`, `.dtypes`, and `.isna().sum()`. Select two columns and clean one missing-value issue while writing down your reason.

Finish by adding a Markdown cell that answers: **What did this example teach
me that I can reuse in a project?**

## Key points

- Learn the concept by running a complete, small example first.
- Predict before execution so your thinking becomes visible.
- Change one thing at a time so cause and effect stay clear.
- Treat errors as clues about the exact line or value Python could not handle.
<!-- fcpython-lab: end -->


## Why this matters

Understand DataFrames, choose relevant data, and handle missing values.

This lesson combines related subtopics that belong together in one learning
conversation. You will still pause for a quiz after each section, but you do not
need to jump between separate pages while building one clear explanation.

::: {.callout-note title="Guiding questions"}
By the end of this lesson, you should be able to answer:

- How do the sections in **Inspecting, Selecting, and Cleaning** fit together?
- Which small example demonstrates each section?
- Which debugging clue should I check first for each section?
:::

::: {.callout-note title="Learning objectives"}
You will practice how to:

- explain the shared concept for this lesson;
- use each section as one step in a larger workflow;
- complete 3 short section quizzes before moving on;
- connect examples, mistakes, and debugging routines.
:::

## Lesson map

- **1. Inspecting DataFrames** — Use shape, columns, dtypes, head, info, and describe to understand data.
- **2. Selecting and Filtering Data** — Choose columns, rows, and conditions from a DataFrame.
- **3. Cleaning Missing Values** — Find and handle missing data deliberately.

## 1. Inspecting DataFrames

Use shape, columns, dtypes, head, info, and describe to understand data.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
Inspection is checking a shipment before unpacking everything: count boxes, read labels,
notice damage.
:::

### What this means

Inspection reveals structure before you transform or model data.

### Example 1
Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({"score": [1, 2, 3], "group": ["a", "a", "b"]})
print(df.shape)
print(df.dtypes)
print(df.describe())
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `import pandas as pd` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.
2. `df = pd.DataFrame({"score": [1, 2, 3], "group": ["a", "a", "b"]})` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.
3. `print(df.shape)` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.
4. `print(df.dtypes)` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.
5. `print(df.describe())` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.callout-note title="Practice"}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy Example 1 into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
Assuming column types from appearance is risky. Always inspect dtypes.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-science-inspecting-dataframes",
  "title": "Inspecting DataFrames checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Inspecting DataFrames?",
      "options": [
        "Inspection reveals structure before you transform or model data.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Inspection reveals structure before you transform or model data."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Inspection is checking a shipment before unpacking everything: count boxes, read labels, notice damage."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after Example 1 works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 2. Selecting and Filtering Data

Choose columns, rows, and conditions from a DataFrame.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
Filtering is like using a sieve: keep rows that match the condition and let others pass
away.
:::

### What this means

Selection narrows a table to relevant columns or rows.

### Example 2
Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({"name": ["Ada", "Grace"], "score": [95, 88]})
high = df[df["score"] >= 90]
print(high[["name", "score"]])
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `import pandas as pd` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.
2. `df = pd.DataFrame({"name": ["Ada", "Grace"], "score": [95, 88]})` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.
3. `high = df[df["score"] >= 90]` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.
4. `print(high[["name", "score"]])` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.callout-note title="Practice"}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy Example 1 into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
Use parentheses around combined conditions in pandas: (a) & (b), not a and b.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-science-selecting-filtering-data",
  "title": "Selecting and Filtering Data checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Selecting and Filtering Data?",
      "options": [
        "Selection narrows a table to relevant columns or rows.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Selection narrows a table to relevant columns or rows."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Filtering is like using a sieve: keep rows that match the condition and let others pass away."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after Example 1 works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## 3. Cleaning Missing Values

Find and handle missing data deliberately.

::: {.callout-tip title="Analogy"}
Missing values are blank cells on a form: you must decide whether to ask again, fill
carefully, or exclude.
:::

### What this means

Missing data are absent values that can affect calculations and conclusions.

### Example 3
Predict what will happen before you run the code.

```python
import pandas as pd

df = pd.DataFrame({"score": [10, None, 30]})
print(df.isna().sum())
print(df.dropna())
```

### Step-by-step explanation

1. `import pandas as pd` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.
2. `df = pd.DataFrame({"score": [10, None, 30]})` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.
3. `print(df.isna().sum())` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.
4. `print(df.dropna())` — pause here and say what this line reads, creates, changes, or displays.

After running the example, compare the actual output with your prediction. If
they differ, do not erase your prediction. The difference is the part that can
teach you the most.

### Challenge

::: {.callout-note title="Practice"}
Change one input value, predict the new output, run the code, and explain the difference
in one sentence.
:::

<details class="solution">
<summary>Show one possible solution path</summary>

1. Copy Example 1 into Colab, Jupyter, or a `.py` file.
2. Mark the line you plan to change.
3. Write a one-sentence prediction.
4. Run the changed code.
5. If the result surprises you, restore the original and change a smaller part.

The goal is not to find the only correct answer. The goal is to create a small
experiment where you can explain cause and effect.

</details>

### Common mistakes

::: {.callout-warning title="Common mistake"}
Dropping missing rows can bias results. Explain your cleaning decision.
:::

When you get stuck, use this debugging routine:

1. Read the last line of the error message or inspect the unexpected output.
2. Find the smallest line of code that could be responsible.
3. Print or inspect the value and type at that point.
4. Change one thing.
5. Run again and record what changed.

### Check your understanding

This quiz checks the ideas in this section before you move on.

<script type="application/json" class="fcpython-ojs-quiz-config">
{
  "id": "data-science-cleaning-missing-values",
  "title": "Cleaning Missing Values checkpoint",
  "instructions": "Choose an answer, then check your work.",
  "questions": [
    {
      "id": "main-idea",
      "prompt": "Which statement best describes Cleaning Missing Values?",
      "options": [
        "Missing data are absent values that can affect calculations and conclusions.",
        "It is mainly about memorizing every Python command before writing code.",
        "It is a topic you can safely skip because later tools replace it.",
        "It is only useful after you already know advanced programming."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The key idea is: Missing data are absent values that can affect calculations and conclusions."
    },
    {
      "id": "analogy",
      "prompt": "What is the purpose of the analogy in this lesson?",
      "options": [
        "To connect a new programming idea to an everyday experience.",
        "To replace practice with memorization.",
        "To prove that programming is only for experts.",
        "To avoid reading and running code examples."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "The analogy in this lesson is: Missing values are blank cells on a form: you must decide whether to ask again, fill carefully, or exclude."
    },
    {
      "id": "debugging-habit",
      "prompt": "What is the best first debugging habit for this topic?",
      "options": [
        "Read the message or output carefully and change one thing at a time.",
        "Delete the whole program immediately.",
        "Change many lines before running again.",
        "Assume Python is random."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Small experiments make cause and effect visible."
    },
    {
      "id": "practice-habit",
      "prompt": "How should you practice after Example 1 works?",
      "options": [
        "Make one small variation, predict the result, run it, and explain what changed.",
        "Copy a large project without reading it.",
        "Skip practice and rely on recognition.",
        "Only read the final key points."
      ],
      "answer_index": 0,
      "explanation": "Prediction plus small variation builds durable understanding."
    }
  ]
}
</script>

{{< include ../_includes/ojs-quiz.qmd >}}

## Notebook and Colab practice

Open a blank notebook at <https://colab.new>. Use one section at a time: copy the
Example 1, predict the result, run it, answer the section quiz, and then move
to the next section. This is better than copying the entire page at once.

## Instructor note

<details class="instructor">
<summary>Teaching notes</summary>

- Treat each section as a short teaching episode.
- Pause for the section quiz before introducing the next section.
- Ask learners to compare sections: what stayed the same, and what changed?
- If time is short, teach the first two sections live and assign the rest as practice.

</details>

## Key points

::: {.callout-tip title="Key points"}
- **Inspecting DataFrames**: Inspection reveals structure before you transform or model data.
- **Selecting and Filtering Data**: Selection narrows a table to relevant columns or rows.
- **Cleaning Missing Values**: Missing data are absent values that can affect calculations and conclusions.
- Use the section quizzes as gates: review before moving on if a quiz feels uncertain.
:::

## References

- pandas documentation: https://pandas.pydata.org/docs/
- seaborn documentation: https://seaborn.pydata.org/
- Matplotlib documentation: https://matplotlib.org/stable/
- Python Tutorial: https://docs.python.org/3/tutorial/
- Quarto OJS documentation: https://quarto.org/docs/interactive/ojs/
- ipywidgets documentation: https://ipywidgets.readthedocs.io/en/stable/
